core_intuitions: A chat template is a deterministic function that flattens role/content pairs into ONE token stream punctuated by special delimiter tokens (im_start/im_end, header_id/eot_id), and it defines a prompt boundary: everything up to the assistant content is prompt, the assistant content is what the model must generate. Train and inference must produce the byte-identical string.

Train-on-completions-only: the loss mask sets labels=-100 on every prompt/role/user/tool token and supervises ONLY assistant content (plus its stop token). In multi-turn dialogue EVERY assistant turn is supervised, not just the last. Masking removes gradient, not context — causal attention still lets each assistant token see all prior tokens.

Padding wastes compute: right-padding every short example up to max_length fills most of each row with dead PAD tokens the model still spends FLOPs on. Sequence packing concatenates several variable-length examples into one fixed-length row, driving utilisation from ~1/4 up toward ~90%.

Cross-document attention leakage is the central correctness hazard of packing: with a plain causal mask, the first token of document 2 can attend to the last token of document 1 — a dependency that never exists at inference. The fix is a block-diagonal causal mask (or Flash-Attention cu_seqlens), which confines attention to within each document, plus per-document position_id resets.

The template families differ only in their delimiter vocabulary and where the system prompt lives; the underlying role→stream structure is identical. apply_chat_template abstracts this so you never hand-concatenate delimiters.

existing.name: chattmpl-multiturn-loss-mask
existing.issue: Renders correctly and legibly in both light and dark themes. Clearly conveys the train-on-completions-only intuition: alternating masked user rows and supervised assistant rows across three turns, with a legend and the crucial caption that masking removes gradient not context. Reading order is clean, no clipping, no theme-color hardcoding problems. This already serves core intuition #2 well; no change needed.

new_figures.name: chattmpl-packing-vs-padding
new_figures.concept: Naive one-example-per-row padding (mostly wasted PAD cells) versus first-fit packing several examples into fixed-length bins, with utilisation contrast.
new_figures.rationale: The whole 'Sequence Packing' section exists to kill padding waste, but the chapter only argues it with prose and a FLOPs formula. A single side-by-side layout makes the waste viscerally obvious and shows WHY concatenating documents into bins recovers it — this is the foundational 'why packing' click that no existing figure serves.
new_figures.placement: Sequence Packing → The Problem with Padding (place the marker right before the Pack-Then-Truncate subsection)
new_figures.brief:
A side-by-side infographic contrasting two ways to batch the same five short examples into fixed-length rows. Use a small illustrative set of documents of unequal length labelled A, B, C, D, E (draw them as horizontal token-strip bars of visibly different lengths; use a neutral/accent fill for real content, distinct from pad). Do NOT invent token counts as benchmark facts — relative bar lengths carry the point; if you label lengths use tiny illustrative integers.

LEFT panel titled 'Naive padding — one example per row'. Draw 5 rows, each the SAME full width = max_length. Each row = one document bar (A..E) left-aligned, with the remainder of the row filled by clearly-different 'PAD' cells (hatched or muted, labelled PAD). Visually the pad region dominates most rows. Under the panel a small utilisation meter: a bar mostly empty with a caption like 'content / total ≈ low' (use a small illustrative fraction such as ~1/4, phrased as illustrative not benchmarked).

RIGHT panel titled 'Packing — concatenate into fixed-length bins'. Draw 2 rows (Bin 1, Bin 2), each again the SAME full width = max_length. Bin 1 = A|B|C concatenated end-to-end with thin document-boundary separators, filling nearly the whole row with only a small trailing PAD sliver. Bin 2 = D|E concatenated, with a larger trailing PAD region (honest: the last bin is under-filled). Under the panel a fuller utilisation meter captioned '≈ high (→ ~90% on real data)' — phrase the 90% as a general tendency, not a measured number.

Between or beneath the panels, one takeaway line: 'Same tokens, far fewer rows — the model stops paying FLOPs for PAD.' Keep document letters consistent across both panels so the eye maps A..E from left layout to right layout. Everything theme-safe (no hardcoded primary colors; use currentColor / theme tokens), all bars and labels inside the viewBox, generous row spacing so PAD labels never overlap document labels. A static frame must fully convey it.

new_figures.name: chattmpl-block-diagonal-attention
new_figures.concept: Attention matrix over a packed 3-document sequence: plain causal triangle (with highlighted cross-document leakage cells) versus block-diagonal causal mask that confines attention within each document.
new_figures.rationale: Cross-document leakage is called out as 'the most important correctness issue with packing', yet it is described only in words and code. An attention-matrix visual is the natural, decisive representation: it shows exactly which query/key cells are illegally allowed and how the block-diagonal mask zeroes them. Motion is unnecessary — the matrix is the intuition, so a crisp static frame is ideal.
new_figures.placement: Sequence Packing → Attention Boundaries: Preventing Cross-Document Leakage (place marker just after the paragraph defining the block-diagonal mask, before the make_block_diagonal_mask code)
new_figures.brief:
Two square attention-matrix grids side by side, rows = query positions, columns = key positions, over the SAME packed sequence of three documents concatenated: Doc1 (a few tokens), Doc2 (a few tokens), Doc3 (a few tokens). Use a small illustrative size, e.g. 3+3+2 = 8 positions, so individual cells are legible. Label the axes with document bands (bracket/label Doc1, Doc2, Doc3 along both the top and the left) and draw thin separator lines at document boundaries so the 3x3 block structure of the grid is visible.

LEFT grid titled 'Plain causal mask (leakage)'. Fill the full lower triangle (j<=i) as 'allowed' cells. Then visually HIGHLIGHT the off-diagonal below-boundary blocks — the cells where a query in Doc2 or Doc3 attends to keys in an EARLIER document (e.g. Doc2 rows attending Doc1 columns, Doc3 rows attending Doc1/Doc2 columns). Mark those highlighted cells distinctly (outline + a small warning tag) and annotate one of them with a callout: 'Doc2 token attends Doc1 — a dependency that never exists at inference.' Upper triangle cells (future) are blocked/blank.

RIGHT grid titled 'Block-diagonal causal mask (fixed)'. Only the three on-diagonal blocks contain allowed cells, and within each block only its own lower triangle (causal within document). All cross-document cells are blocked/blank. Add a short caption: 'Attention confined to each document; position_ids also reset per doc.'

Beneath both, a one-line note: 'Flash-Attention cu_seqlens = [0,3,6,8] encodes these boundaries without materialising the matrix.' (use cu_seqlens consistent with the illustrative 3+3+2 split). Use a simple two-state cell encoding: allowed (filled) vs blocked (empty), with the leakage cells getting an extra highlight state — keep it to at most three visually distinct cell states so it stays readable. Theme-safe fills via theme tokens / currentColor, no hardcoded primaries; keep every label and bracket inside the viewBox; ensure the highlight color reads in both light and dark. Fully understandable as a static frame.

new_figures.name: chattmpl-template-anatomy
new_figures.concept: Anatomy of one rendered conversation turn: special delimiter tokens vs plain role string vs content, the prompt boundary, and the appended generation prompt at inference.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter opens on the idea that a template maps roles to a single delimited token stream with a prompt boundary, and everything downstream (masking, EOS handling, family differences) rests on distinguishing special tokens from ordinary text. A labelled anatomy of a single ChatML turn grounds that vocabulary once, so the later masking and family-comparison material lands. Serves core intuition #1, which no current figure isolates.
new_figures.placement: The Major Template Families → ChatML (place marker right after the two-turn ChatML example block)
new_figures.brief:
A horizontal 'token ribbon' dissecting one complete assistant turn in ChatML, reading left to right as a sequence of adjacent token chips: [<|im_start|>] [assistant] [\n] [content tokens …] [<|im_end|>] [\n]. Give the three categories three distinct chip styles and a small legend: (a) SPECIAL delimiter tokens — <|im_start|>, <|im_end|> — emphasised as single indivisible vocabulary entries; (b) ROLE STRING — the word 'assistant' + newline — marked 'ordinary text, tokenised normally, NOT a special token'; (c) CONTENT — the actual reply tokens.

Above the ribbon show a compact preceding context (a collapsed user turn drawn as a faded strip ending in <|im_end|>) so the reader sees this assistant turn in place. Draw a vertical 'prompt boundary' line falling exactly between the role header (<|im_start|>assistant\n) and the first content token, labelled 'prompt boundary — everything left is prompt (masked), everything right is generated (supervised)'. This visually ties the delimiter anatomy to the loss-mask figure that follows.

Below the ribbon add a small inset labelled 'at inference' showing the server appends just the header '<|im_start|>assistant\n' after the last user turn and the model decodes content until it emits <|im_end|>. Keep it to a single turn so chips stay large and readable; render the literal token strings monospace. Theme-safe styling via theme tokens/currentColor, the three chip styles must remain distinguishable in both light and dark, all chips and the boundary label inside the viewBox with no overlap. Static frame conveys everything.

summary: Chapter is concept-rich (templates, loss masking, packing, cross-document attention) but figure-poor: one strong existing figure covers multi-turn loss masking. Keep it as-is (renders well in both themes). Add three static infographics for the unserved core intuitions: (1) packing vs padding utilisation — the 'why packing' click; (2) block-diagonal attention matrix — the central correctness hazard, best shown as a static attention grid with highlighted leakage cells; (3) a template-turn anatomy grounding special-vs-ordinary tokens and the prompt boundary that the whole chapter rests on. No animation warranted — every intuition here is captured by a single well-designed frame.
